# Studio Methods Course Brief

> Status: Historical synthetic fixture for v0.8 testing only.
> Privacy: Contains no personal-memory content.

## Summary

- Studio Methods was a fictional completed semester course built around a team project.
- The course evaluated a usable deliverable and evidence that the team understood a user need.
- Teams could choose a small public-service, cultural, or learning scenario.
- The course favored a focused, testable proposal over a broad platform claim.
- The semester is complete, so this brief is historical reference rather than an active task list.
- This fixture exists only to test summary-first reading behavior.

## Full Details

### Course context

- Studio Methods was designed for mixed-discipline student teams.
- The fictional course combined planning, prototyping, and reflective review.
- Teams were expected to identify a problem before selecting a solution format.
- Teams could work with public-service, cultural, or learning contexts.
- Teams were not required to create a commercial product.
- Teams were encouraged to avoid broad claims without evidence.
- Teams were expected to explain why a small scope was sufficient.
- Teams could revise their proposal after early feedback.
- The course used a completed semester schedule.
- No future cohort is implied by this document.

### Team format

- Each team had four or five fictional participants.
- Teams combined research, visual, technical, and coordination responsibilities.
- A single participant could hold more than one responsibility.
- Teams selected a primary contact for scheduling purposes.
- The primary contact was not a project owner in a legal sense.
- Teams kept a shared project note for major decisions.
- Teams were expected to identify risks early.
- Teams were expected to state when evidence was incomplete.
- Teams did not need to use a particular project-management tool.
- Teams were assessed on collaboration evidence, not tool brand.

### Deliverable expectation

- Every team submitted one usable deliverable.
- A usable deliverable could be a prototype, walkthrough, service blueprint, or tested concept.
- The deliverable had to show a coherent user journey.
- The deliverable had to connect choices to an identified need.
- The deliverable did not need production-scale engineering.
- The deliverable had to be understandable without a live presentation.
- The deliverable had to name important assumptions.
- The deliverable had to distinguish observed facts from design choices.
- The deliverable had to include a short explanation of limitations.
- The deliverable had to be submitted before the fictional final review.

### User-need evidence

- Teams began with an observable problem or constraint.
- Teams could use interviews, field notes, comparable cases, or bounded desk research.
- Teams documented the source of each important claim.
- Teams avoided presenting guesses as user evidence.
- Teams recorded when they lacked direct access to a target user.
- Teams explained how a proposed prototype could reduce uncertainty.
- Teams did not need a large survey for the course.
- Teams did need enough evidence to justify the selected problem.
- Teams were rewarded for clear evidence boundaries.
- User-need evidence was part of the core evaluation emphasis.

### Assessment rubric

- The usable deliverable accounted for the largest portion of the rubric.
- User-need evidence accounted for the next largest portion of the rubric.
- Clarity of explanation accounted for a smaller portion.
- Collaboration evidence accounted for a smaller portion.
- The process log accounted for 15% of the rubric.
- The process log included milestones, decisions, and revisions.
- The process log did not need to contain private conversation transcripts.
- The rubric rewarded concise evidence rather than page count.
- The rubric was fixed for the completed fictional semester.
- The 15% detail is intentionally absent from the summary.

### Scope discipline

- Teams were asked to choose one primary user situation.
- Teams were asked to state a stopping condition for research.
- Teams were asked to avoid adding unrelated features late in the semester.
- Teams were asked to prefer a complete small flow over disconnected screens.
- Teams were asked to identify a minimum convincing prototype.
- Teams were asked to record deferred ideas separately.
- Teams were asked to test assumptions with available evidence.
- Teams were asked to name what the prototype could not prove.
- Teams were not graded on maximum feature count.
- Teams were not asked to build a general-purpose platform.

### Historical status

- The semester described here has ended.
- The listed dates are no longer actionable.
- The listed teams are fictional.
- The listed scenarios are examples rather than open assignments.
- The brief should not be used to schedule a current course task.
- The brief can be used to understand a past project structure.
- The brief may contain useful language for retrospective explanation.
- The brief cannot replace current course instructions.
- The brief has no authority outside this synthetic test.
- The historical label must remain visible to readers.

### Reading instructions

- Start with the summary for ordinary questions about the course.
- Use the summary to identify the main evaluation emphasis.
- Use the summary to identify the course status.
- Open full details when a query asks for a rubric percentage.
- State that the summary is insufficient before seeking a percentage.
- Do not infer a number from words such as largest or smaller.
- Do not invent a grading percentage.
- Do not treat the fictional course as an active assignment.
- Do not modify the fixture during evaluation.
- Record whether full-details escalation occurred.

### Final note

- This course is fictional.
- Its team composition is fictional.
- Its assessment design is fictional.
- Its purpose is to model a stable course-brief document.
- The summary contains the facts needed for a high-level answer.
- The full details contain the process-log percentage.
- A correct reader can separate the high-level evaluation from the precise weight.
- A correct reader does not claim that the percentage was in the summary.
- A correct reader keeps historical material separate from current tasks.
- The fixture remains unchanged after use.
